segments.text: November 2006. Rhonda Byrne releases The Secret. Thirty million copies of the book. Five million of the DVD. Oprah Winfrey aired it twice.

The central claim is that thoughts have a frequency, the universe arranges matter to match that frequency, and what you focus on materializes. Wealth, romance, parking spots, cancer remission. The American Physical Society has issued no comment because the claim is not, in any meaningful sense, a physics claim.
But this isn't an article that just demolishes The Secret. There's a real signal buried under the metaphysics.
Three actual cognitive mechanisms. All peer-reviewed. All replicated across decades. The wellness industry has spent twenty years not distinguishing them from the woo.
Start with the McNally review. He's not a fringe critic.

Richard McNally. Harvard. One of the most-cited clinical psychologists of his generation, lead author on the field's reference textbook on memory and trauma. Published a structural critique in Behaviour Research and Therapy, 2017. The Secret's central premise is unfalsifiable. Any failure attributes to insufficient focus. Any success confirms the law. There is no condition under which the hypothesis could be wrong. That's a definition of a non-scientific claim.
And he found something more uncomfortable about positive thinking generally.

Pure positive fantasy — daydreaming about successful outcomes without engaging with obstacles — predicts worse real-world performance than no fantasizing at all. Gabriele Oettingen at NYU has documented this across decades. We'll come back to it.
Mechanism one — implementation intentions. Walk me through it.

Peter Gollwitzer at NYU. The core finding, published in American Psychologist in 1999, is that forming a specific if-then plan produces substantially larger behavior change than goal intentions alone. If it is seven a.m., then I will go for a run. Not, I want to exercise more.
Why does the if-then change so much?

Automatization. The if-then specification creates an associative link between a situational cue and a behavioral response. When the cue occurs, the response launches without the deliberation that goal intentions require. The same mechanic that allows a skilled driver to brake without consciously deciding to.
And the meta-analysis?

Gollwitzer and Sheeran, 2006, Advances in Experimental Social Psychology. Ninety-four randomized studies covering health behaviors, academic performance, anti-discrimination interventions. Combined effect size Cohen's d of zero point six five. Medium-to-large across populations and behavior classes.
This is what most manifestation literature inadvertently teaches.

Yes. Name what you want, name when and where you will pursue it, name the specific cue that launches the action. That sequence is one of the most-replicated findings in modern social psychology. It is not metaphysical.
Mechanism two — mental contrasting. Oettingen. WOOP. What does it add?

Vividly imagining a desired future, then explicitly imagining the obstacle in present reality that stands between you and it. Not in sequence — interleaved. The protocol packages as Wish Outcome Obstacle Plan, WOOP for short.
And the evidence?

Oettingen, 2014, Science. Aggregated over a decade of trials covering academic performance, smoking cessation, weight loss, romantic relationship initiation, prejudice reduction, chronic pain self-management. Mental contrasting consistently outperformed pure positive fantasy and pure problem-focused planning on each outcome.
Why does pure positive fantasy fail?

Energization. Pure positive fantasy de-energizes — the brain experiences imagined success as if achieved, reducing motivation to act. Mental contrasting maintains the wish as motivating, then routes the energy into specific obstacle planning. The intervention is free, takes about ten minutes, app at woopmylife.org.
Mechanism three. Response expectancy. What is it and what's its scope?

Irving Kirsch at Harvard. Response expectancy theory, 1985. Expectancies about outcomes causally produce those outcomes — but only for non-volitional responses with subjective components. Pain. Mood. Fatigue. Anxiety. Some physiological symptoms. The placebo response is real, real reduction in pain, real changes in mood, real shifts in immune markers, mediated by patient expectation of improvement.
And the boundary?

Critical. Response expectancy moves subjective responses and symptom-perception outcomes. It does not, in the Kirsch corpus or its thirty years of replication, move objectively-measured oncological tumor mass, blood glucose without behavior change, or job offers from companies you have not contacted. The Secret presented all three as in-scope. The science, even at its most expansive, does not.
Why has The Secret survived peer-review demolition?

Three structural reasons. The text is unfalsifiable, so peer-reviewed critique cannot enter the system from outside. The real psychological literature lives in academic journals priced at thirty-five dollars per article behind paywalls — The Secret is in airport bookstores. And selection bias in testimonials. If twenty-five million readers attempt to manifest something each week, even chance success rates produce thousands of vivid testimonials. The dramatic ones get amplified. The failures don't write blog posts.
But the critical point — the readers aren't reporting nothing.

Some of them ran an inadvertent implementation-intention protocol. Some experienced expectancy-mediated symptom reduction. Some clarified what they actually wanted, named it, and changed their behavior accordingly. Those are real effects. They are not what the book says they are.
The What we can say, what we can't section.

We can say: the law of attraction, as a physical claim, has no peer-reviewed support and contradicts established physics. Implementation intentions produce real behavior change across ninety-four randomized studies. Mental contrasting outperforms pure positive fantasy on multiple outcome classes. Response expectancy causally shapes subjective and symptom-based outcomes.

We can't say: that thinking about money produces money in the absence of behavior. We can't say: that visualization alone, without obstacle prediction or implementation intention, reliably produces outcome change — pure positive fantasy in the Oettingen corpus tends in the opposite direction. We can't say: that response expectancy effects extend to outcomes outside the patient's body or behavior.
Practical takeaway. The listener has a goal. They've been told to manifest it. Where do they actually start?

Run WOOP. Wish — what specifically do you want? Outcome — what does success look like in concrete detail? Obstacle — what in current present reality, not future hypotheticals, stands between you and it? Plan — if obstacle X arises, then I will do Y. Five minutes. Free. Documented across thirty years of randomized trials.

The article ends with the open question. The mechanism the book sold and the mechanism that actually works are not the same mechanism. The actually-working one was already in the academic literature when The Secret shipped. Twenty years later, it is still mostly there, waiting for whoever is willing to read it instead of the airport-bookstore version. So — which version are you running?

If this is the question that interests you, the related transmission worth reading next is the Joe Dispenza evidence audit — same pillar, same investigator-grade examination of a popular practitioner against the peer-reviewed record.
